title: Monolith to Microservices
subtitle: Evolutionary Patterns to Transform Your Monolith
author: Sam Newman
excerpt: A pragmatic, pattern-rich guide to incrementally transforming monolithic systems into microservices. Sam Newman complements his classic Building Microservices with a hands-on playbook for the migration itself — when to do it, when not to, how to find seams, how to split the data layer, and how to handle the organizational change that determines whether the migration succeeds or fails.
keyIdeas: You should probably not migrate — most monoliths should stay. The bar is clear, evidence-based, and explicitly contrarian
The Strangler Fig Pattern — incrementally replace a monolith by growing a new system around it, redirecting traffic piece by piece until the old system can be retired
Find seams in the monolith along natural business boundaries; bounded contexts from Domain-Driven Design are the canonical way to identify them
Decompose by capability, not by layer — services organized around business capabilities outperform services organized around technical layers (presentation, business logic, data)

The data layer is the hardest part of migration — shared databases and distributed transactions are the most common failure modes, and the patterns to address them (change data capture, event collaboration, branch by abstraction) are the deepest in the book
Event collaboration (services publish events, others react) and event sourcing (state derived from a log of events) are alternatives to shared databases and synchronous calls
UI composition — the back end is rarely the only monolith; a composite UI pattern lets front ends pull from multiple services cleanly without resurrecting coupling
Organizational change is the binding constraint — Conway's Law means team structure determines architecture; migration and team restructuring must proceed together
Prerequisites are not optional — you need CI/CD, observability, containerization, and a deployment culture before microservice migration makes sense
Anti-patterns are explicit and named — distributed monoliths, microservice envy, shared databases, and chatty services are recurring failure modes Newman has watched destroy real projects
keyTakeaways: The default answer to 'should we migrate to microservices?' is no. Newman's rule: if the monolith works, keep it. Migration is justified only by clear, measured pain
The Strangler Fig is the dominant migration pattern. You do not rewrite monoliths — you grow replacements around them, route traffic gradually, and remove the old code when nothing calls it
Find your seams before you write any code. Bounded contexts, event storming, and the seams in your existing module structure tell you where the cuts should be
Decompose by capability. A 'Customer' service beats a 'Database' service. Capability-aligned services are autonomous, independently deployable, and survive organizational change
Shared databases are a trap. Two services writing to the same table is not microservices — it is a distributed monolith. Migrate the data deliberately, one service at a time
Synchronous calls between services are a smell. Event collaboration, change data capture, and eventual consistency handle 90% of what you think requires a join
UI composition is often skipped and always regretted. If your front end is a monolith calling microservices, you have moved the problem, not solved it
Migrate in waves, not in one big bang. Each wave extracts a service, runs both in parallel, verifies behaviour, and only then retires the old code
Team structure and architecture structure must co-evolve. Reverse Conway works: change the teams first, and the service boundaries will follow
You cannot migrate without CI/CD, containerization, central logging, distributed tracing, and feature flags. If you do not have these, build them first
whoShouldRead: Senior engineers and tech leads about to undertake or already executing a migration from monolith to microservices
Software architects deciding whether, when, and how to break apart a legacy system
Engineering managers and directors whose team structure is blocking or enabling the migration
Platform engineers building the CI/CD, observability, and deployment infrastructure that makes migration possible
Consultants and principal engineers who advise organizations on modernization programs
whoShouldSkip: Engineers at greenfield startups with no monolith yet — read Building Microservices first, or save this book for when you inherit one
Anyone looking for a microservices primer — this book assumes you already know why you might want them; it covers the how
Pure infrastructure engineers — the book focuses on architecture and organization, not Kubernetes, service meshes, or specific tools
Readers seeking a research-paper treatment of distributed systems theory — Newman writes for practitioners, not academics
